focus: Gemini CLI deep dive — instruction-based context loading, GEMINI.md auto-loading, MCP parity, and UX comparison vs Claude/OpenCode/Codex
noveltyJustification: 6 of 8 findings are fully new (official GEMINI.md hierarchy, AGENTS.md configurability, upstream Gemini hooks, .mcp.json non-support, trigger design, parity scoring); 2 refine earlier non-hook-runtime assumptions by separating upstream Gemini capability from the repo's current Gemini setup.
keyQuestions: Does Gemini CLI auto-load GEMINI.md or AGENTS.md?
Can Gemini access the same MCP servers as the other runtimes?
What instruction pattern best approximates Claude-style context loading?
answeredQuestions: Gemini auto-loads GEMINI.md hierarchically and can also load AGENTS.md via context.fileName
Gemini can use the same four MCP servers, but via .gemini/settings.json mcpServers rather than .mcp.json
Instruction-only Gemini can approximate Claude-style context loading at about 75% parity; with native Gemini hooks the ceiling rises to about 88%
ruledOut: Treating .mcp.json as an official Gemini CLI config surface
Treating Gemini as hookless at the upstream product level
Claiming instruction files alone can deterministically enforce startup priming

focus: COPILOT CLI DEEP DIVE — instruction auto-loading, recovery reuse, and session-start auto-trigger design for memory_context + code_graph_scan
noveltyJustification: This pass adds four concrete corrections to prior runtime assumptions: official Copilot CLI auto-loads AGENTS.md/.github/copilot-instructions.md/.github/instructions rather than a .github/copilot/ directory, repository-level custom agents live under .github/agents, repo-local .agents/agents is not the authoritative startup surface, and the right place for session-start graph priming is shared instruction files rather than optional agent profiles. Two findings deepen prior work by translating Claude recovery steps into Copilot-safe trigger patterns and by separating semantic vs structural auto-triggers.
keyQuestions: Does Copilot CLI auto-load any instruction files?
How should Copilot auto-trigger memory_context and code_graph_scan on session start?
How does Copilot's markdown agent model change integration versus Codex TOML agents?
answeredQuestions: Copilot CLI auto-loads AGENTS.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .github/instructions/**/*.instructions.md, optional root CLAUDE.md/GEMINI.md, and local/home instruction files
This repo currently has no .github/copilot/ directory or Copilot instruction files; the existing universal AGENTS.md already supplies Gate 1 memory_match_triggers but not code-graph startup priming

Copilot session-start priming should live in AGENTS.md plus optional .github/copilot-instructions.md or path-specific instructions, with code_graph_scan gated by code_graph_status and structural-intent triggers rather than fired on every turn

Copilot's markdown agent profiles are selection/delegation surfaces, whereas Codex TOML agents are canonical runtime prompts in this repo, so global startup behavior should not rely on Copilot agent profiles alone
ruledOut: Using .github/copilot/ as the primary Copilot CLI auto-load surface
Relying on optional Copilot agent profiles alone for universal session-start priming
Running code_graph_scan unconditionally on every Copilot prompt

focus: VERIFY review P1 findings 4-6 against current code: stop-time auto-save surrogate, structural endLine collapse, and code_graph_context seed identity stripping
noveltyJustification: Primarily a verification pass. All three review findings remain valid in current code; the main new value is tightening the exact failure mode for each boundary: stop-hook surrogate state reuse, still-unfixed one-line ranges, and public-handler seed type erasure.

Claiming the endLine fix landed after Segment 6

focus: VERIFY review P1 findings 1-3 against current compact-recovery, persistence, and stop-hook code
noveltyJustification: This pass converts three pending review claims into current-code verdicts: P1-1 and P1-2 remain real in today's implementation, while P1-3 does not match the current or recorded session-stop guard logic.
keyQuestions: Does pendingCompactPrime get cleared too early in session-prime?
Do hook-state persistence failures still present as successful saves?
Does a stop-hook recursion guard based on isAutoSurface skip normal stop work?
answeredQuestions: P1-1: Yes. The payload is cleared before stdout injection completes, although it is first copied into local variables.
P1-2: Yes. saveState still returns void and swallows write failures, so callers cannot distinguish failed persistence from success.
P1-3: No. Current session-stop logic uses stop_hook_active, not isAutoSurface, so this finding is a false positive against current code.
ruledOut: P1-1 subclaim that pendingCompactPrime is nulled before session-prime reads it
P1-3 claim that a current isAutoSurface recursion guard blocks ordinary Stop execution

focus: VERIFY review P1-10 and key P2 findings against current code (rootDir validation, compact cache freshness, workingSet reachability, transcript cache accounting, exception text exposure)
noveltyJustification: This pass fully revalidated the headline security and correctness findings against current source and added two important nuances: the startup Working Memory branch is unreachable through the normal hook pipeline rather than logically impossible, and cache buckets are parsed correctly but dropped only from surfaced totals and cost estimates.
keyQuestions: Do review findings P1-10, P2-1, P2-2, P2-3, and P2-8 still match the current implementation?
answeredQuestions: code_graph_scan still accepts caller-controlled rootDir with no workspace-boundary validation
session-prime still reuses pendingCompactPrime without any freshness check
the startup Working Memory branch still has no in-repo workingSet producer
transcript totals and surfaced USD estimates still exclude cacheCreation and cacheRead buckets
memory_context and code_graph_context still reflect raw internal exception text back to callers
ruledOut: Treating session-stop's duplicate-save age check as protection for compact recovery freshness
Treating the Working Memory branch as produced anywhere in the current hook pipeline
Treating cache buckets as absent from transcript parsing

focus: CROSS-RUNTIME UX PARITY MATRIX -- definitive OpenCode/Codex/Copilot/Gemini comparison vs Claude Code
noveltyJustification: Mostly a synthesis pass, but it adds one important clarification not made explicit in the per-runtime deep dives: Copilot's current repo state combines strong auto-loaded instruction surfaces with partial native hook support, placing it above pure instruction-only OpenCode/Codex in current-state parity, while Gemini has the highest non-Claude ceiling but the weakest local configuration today.
keyQuestions: What is the definitive current-state parity matrix across OpenCode, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and Gemini CLI?

What are the top 3 highest-leverage cross-runtime improvements?

Treating Copilot and Gemini as equivalent to fully hookless runtimes
Treating optional agent profiles as sufficient for universal startup behavior
